entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Well, good morning as we thank you for. For joining us today.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Thank you.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Please let us know which initiative we're going to be doing today. And before you describe how was the experience of interviewing them, maybe just tell us how was the contact, initial contact, and the process to get to the interview.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay, so we'll be debriefing Food Click Living Lab. That's what it's called. And they were very easy to contact because we already had the initial contact, Dan, who is the project manager for Foodclick in Aarhus Municipality. So this was a very easy case to get rolling. Yeah. And he. He had enough time. Well, barely, but he made the time for us, which was nice. An overview of what this is. It's a project run by the eu and it is kind of in the word itself, it's Living labs within eight different cities, where OH is one of them. And so it's essentially a research study lab that has these eight different cities where they collect data and analyze data and they help them make interventions and implement them to transform the food systems towards more sustainability. And within ohuz, it's a collaboration between the municipality and Aarhus University and then whoever the stakeholders are that they need to collaborate with and collect data from. So the goal of Food Click Living Lab is essentially to try and transform city food systems through whole system use. So this is by involving all stakeholders from the whole value chain within the city's food systems. Yeah, and the university is quite a key component here as well, because it's helping the communication between the food labs kind of main offices, but also helping the municipality be rigorous in the data collection and. Yeah, creating these interventions. And so if I were to put some. Some words to describe the interview, what I think was like, the main learning from this was that it's a project that's been running for about three years now, and it's going to end in 20. In 27. So it does have like a hard end point. And through this period there are like two stages. First, they've been collecting the data and analyzing, and now they're on to the second stage, which is this intervention creation and implementation. And in 2017, the funding is going to end.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: So it's funding 2027, right?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, exactly. So the funding is funded by the eu, the funding is going to end and then the interventions might end as well. So it's very dependent on how the stakeholders and how the food system in these local regions like the interventions, and also how they view them, if they want to continue them or not. And this was something that Dan talked about a fair bit that it's super interesting to be a part of. But he's not sure if this will be continued after 2017. No, sorry, 2027. Yeah. So the ultimate goal of transforming the food system is not necessary to be fulfilled, but what they really need is to have political support and public funding after this time period, otherwise it'll kind of come to a halt. And so that was. That was fairly interesting. And obviously that I think the municipality's goal is to integrate this kind of thinking within the policymaking and they can see how food Click as a project has already now contributes to a different way of looking at food and food systems within the city. So when they started the project, food wasn't on the agenda as like one sustainability theme. It was kind of forgotten in between like transportation and energy and yeah, these kind of different things. And now in the new climate plan they have food as one theme of its own. And the way of thinking about food as something that if you change food habits and if you change the system, it has co benefits and to society in general is something it's a way of thinking that they're implementing now as well. So he was highlighting this change in diet as being super beneficial, both for health but also for climate. And this is. Yeah, this is a change in thought that has come from Food Click as a project. So that was interesting. Seems like they are changing the food system already. And a bit about the business model. As I said, it's funded by the EU and essentially always municipality deliver data and functions as a living lab for experimentation on how to change the food system. So success is both measured on the impact on the system, but also also the lessons taken. So this is like knowledge production as well that will be taken forward within the eu. And I think something important to note about this project in general is these competing motives. So we've got like three major players within it. We've got the EU and the municipality and then Osh University. And from what I could gather, the EU and the university is quite focused on the knowledge production. And obviously the EU wants to transform the food system as well, and the university wants to as well. But I would say that the municipality is more about like serving the community and upholding this goal of being carbon neutral by 2030. So they have at least this was what he was saying. They're closer to the lived reality of their community and the stakeholders. So there's sometimes a bit of a conflict between what is thought and theorized by the academic world versus what is what is really feasible in reality. And this is a bit of a, not a conflict necessarily, but a misalignment sometimes that he struggles with when he has to like implement the things that Food Click is helping him develop. It's not always actually possible and people don't always want to do it. Yeah. And during, during the interview. Wait, I actually wanna. I wanna just skip that question and go to the next one quickly and then come back. Yeah. Because this, what I just said kind of relates to what you, you should be on the lookout for in two years. So obviously we will be at the end of the project and I think something interesting to talk to them about is how the progress on this implementation stage has gone, so how the community has received these different interventions and how Food Click has been able to support, but also what FoodClick has gained from this and what their future goals with the knowledge. And yeah, the implementation stage generally can do for their, for their policy or whatever they want to do in the future. I think something very important to look out for is kind of the political landscape around the municipality because I think Food Click will, as it has, affect the political landscape, but also the other way around. So Dan was highlighting how if he doesn't have the political support and the support of the community, these interventions won't really go anywhere. So they will end with the project. And he would obviously love for the interventions to keep going, but he's going to need both funding but also support to be able to do this. I think one indicator of this could be one of the interventions that they're doing right now, which is a collaboration between schools and farms. So they're doing a project where they're connecting children to farms essentially and doing a bit of a course where children understand where their food comes from and they're able to like, get out into nature and connect to nature in a different way. And I think if this is something that will be implemented in schools on a longer term basis, that will really mean that Food Clique has done something good for the community and something. Yeah. Changed something that they found would actually change the food system in general. So, yeah, that's probably a good indicator of success, maybe something to study as well. In relation to like, what other cases of stakeholders could we consider studying could be these institutions that are trying to. Yeah. Collaborate with the municipality. Yeah. And then in terms of kind of what was unexpected or any difficulties I encountered, I will say time was a bit of a constraint, restraint here. So he didn't have loads of time. We did get through a lot of the questions and I think the interview kind of covers everything that you guys need. But there are definitely. Yeah, there could have been more time. I'll say we had an hour and a half. But it's obviously a complicated case and a complicated project, so there's a lot to say. Another thing, Dan mentioned it a bit and I could hear that there were some kind of team conflicts. So I wonder if this is one of the things that will also affect the success of the project. He was saying how the food cult know the work culture wasn't the best where he worked. So that was kind of unexpected. I know that this was why he had to cut it short as well because they're working on increasing kind of.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: The collaboration between the teams. But that was not apparently the best. Yeah. And besides these insights, I think this is the main things that I took from it and would obviously love. Any questions you've got.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Good. Yes, I do have a couple. Let's start with the end because I think that's for me listening to you, there's a couple of things that make me wonder like if this is going to be impactful or not. Because it kind of has the potential of being meso, not only because of where it is. Right. It's placed in the government which has, as you presented at the beginning of the conversation, you know, the breath throughout the entire processes in the city. City. And this is a city we're very interested in. We've seen interesting cases with you of what's happening there, etc. So I'll have a quick question and then maybe longer one. And it's why the UEU picked Arhus is one of the cities for food. Click. This was a public application that the city decided to pursue because from what you were saying, they're not. They were not very interested in food as a topic before this. So I wonder. And because we've seen so many interesting things happening there, maybe that's something you discussed with him or you know.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, I did discuss the beginning of the case, like how it. How it came about and it was apparently on the initiative of the university. So old university wanted to become a part of this project and they were kind of, I think the initiators of this. So I don't think the municipality necessarily was in on it from the beginning, but they've definitely gotten there. He didn't say a lot about it. This was the main bit of information he said about the beginning of this.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: That started as a like university initiative. Probably if you are going to Operate those funds for public policy you need like the backing of the city. And then let's discuss a bit about Dan.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Like, is he a public servant? Does he. Like, he. Was he doing different things before starting doing this? Like, how does he operate within that particular system and what kind of agency he might have to. To, you know, leverage other resources in the future or. How do you see that?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. So he comes from an academic background, so it makes sense why he would want to be a part of the research project like this. He is employed by the municipalities of a public servant, but he is the project manager of Food Click within the municipality. So he does have a bit of a dual role. He both has to collaborate with all the cities that are in the project and the Food Click's main research team, but also the municipality. And he would probably place himself more in the municipality role as someone who takes care of the city's motivations generally to serve the community. But I know personally for him, he has worked with sustainability before. He's worked with food. He has a PhD. I forget exactly what. But he is academic by training, so he. He. It seemed like for him it was kind of a. A difficult, like, personal project for him as well, because he really has a lot of drive in changing things to become more sustainable. And for him it becomes frustrating when things move fairly slowly within the municipality. So it seems like. It seems like he has to uphold his job description with the municipality, but wants to move things quicker with. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: And in terms of staffing, like, the people that are working there, are they also more researchers? More like they were public workers in other. Doing other stuff mix.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Well, it's a fairly complicated kind of team. Right. Because you've got his team, which is the public servant team, and they work for him, or he's the project manager of him of them. And then you have the academics on the Aarhus University side that kind of work in their own team and then you have the Food Click main offices. Yeah, I don't know if that answers the question.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Yes, no, I'm wondering about like the team structure and the organizational structure, because since this is a contained project with a. With a limited deadline, as you were saying, like, I mean, because of what you mentioned, what we've seen, what I've seen personally as a public servant, these things move fairly slowly, you know, and have like five years in which you plan for three and execute for two and then hope these final two really generate some capacity, that's going to be very difficult. So I'm trying to understand if there's something there other than dance thrive that really can mobilize.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Okay. Yes. I want to say, well, a person that you guys in socioscope knows as well, Bender Karma, she works not with Dan, but alongside him. And they will go out, for example, on workshop days and research days with the stakeholders out in the community. So he's not the only one driving the sustainability agenda within the municipality. And I think this is why it's gaining traction as well. But within Food Click as like one case or one project, he would be the one who has the motivating drive within the municipality and he is really going to need support from the community and from the politicians, otherwise this is just going to end. And that was kind of, I think, what was fairly frustrating for him as well, that if these interventions aren't really well thought out and go really well, then they're probably going to end. I obviously know that the university and Food Click will have a lot of drive and motivation for this to actually go far, but I don't think they have the same power to enable and help the community after these five years. So.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: And there's no way to either renew. So does he have like, has he thought about it, like, you know, what's happening after 2027 or he's just completely focused in trying to design things that might stick and hope they would stick on their own without them. That is a program to support.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, that's, that's the thought. He has thought about it a lot and he, he seemed, don't want to see, say, deflated, but he didn't seem too optimistic about being able to do anything but that. So I, I got the sense that this was his only option and therefore he was going to work as hard as he could to make that option work. And obviously you have some kind of motivating factors, like the fact that right now they have food as their own theme within the new climate plan and the very ambitious goal of being, of being carbon like zero in 2030. So the community will have to do something and food is going to be an important theme. So there are things to believe or things to kind of encourage him. Yeah. Or to think that the community will bring this forward. But it did seem like that was his only, only option at the moment, unfortunately.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Okay, can you maybe go deeper regarding like, stakeholders, you know, so we do have the. You who's funding the city, some public servants in the city that have a lot of authority, like Bendy. Right. You were mentioning communities, the university, but is there anyone else that's Interesting. Their providers have theirs started working directly with communities?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, I actually think there are some details around this international collaboration between the cities that's quite important for the municipality itself and also for the project. So he was talking about this very lightly in the beginning, but when we started talking about transactions, he kind of dove further into how the collaborations actually worked really well. So because the other eight cities have large influential cities within the eu, so for example, Berlin and Amsterdam and Barcelona, this project for, yeah, for owus has become a part of becoming kind of more influential and kind of legitimizing their status as a sustainability city. And the collaboration with the bigger cities also really provided them with a lot of contacts and a lot of momentum in the more global transformation within food. So I thought that was like, that's quite an important part of this as well because it connects people across Europe to be able to kind of help everyone transition at the same time. And also he was talking personally about how this is a difficult fight to fight alone and was emphasizing how when you have other people around you, this, this can become like a venting space as well. Like, oh, it's annoying that the politicians aren't helping, but when you are able to vent, you're also able to kind of keep your motivation. At least that was what he said. So this collaboration is super important. And another stakeholder that he mentioned here was the local, the local stakeholders. And so this has enabled him to get out into the community. And he said that sometimes the municipalities will be looked at as this giant that sits in the big offices and decides about everything that goes on in the city and is quite far removed from the lived reality of, for example, a newly started farm or producer. So for him to be able to go out and talk to these stakeholders, he's been able to kind of like demystify or create a more positive way of looking at the municipality within the community. So this has been very important also while showing face, but also just talking to them about what their expectations are for the next five years, like until they have to be net zero and yeah, I don't know, like, get them all on board with what they're doing and gain support from them. And he was talking about how feeling heard was very important for the community and he's been able to do this for them. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Good. Let me think about something that we might be missing. So in terms of output, did he mention particularly like an interesting intervention that they've already thought about, like to get a sense of what they're. Because, I mean, you've been very descriptive of what they do and how they do it, but we really don't know what they're actually going to be doing. Is this more kind of behavioral, like nudging or like subsidies or like, how does this look like? Do you have an idea?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. Um, so he was talking about two themes as the major themes that kind of guide theme interventions. So one of them was that he wanted to. Or food Click was advising him to try and connect the system more, which was why they started this children and farm intervention. So this is a specific intervention they're going to be running and they're going to collect data and figure out how this impacted the children's understanding of farm, understanding of food and nature. And I think they're already running with this now. So this is a specific one that you can kind of measure the impact of when you go back in two years. And the other theme was to change consumer behavior. So this is quite. Behavioral interventions, like. And this comes from their Scope 3 admissions, how they need to reduce like everyone's admissions. But they're really focusing on the consumer side of this. And from my understanding they're getting a lot of advice from the EU that try trying to adapt consumer behavior is just the way forward. Like this is what they need to do. So they're trying to reduce specifically food. No, sorry, meat consumption. And this is something that they're going to be measuring on as well and will be a success criteria for them. If they've reduced the meat consumption, then that will be. Yeah, a major thing. So this is something you can measure on as well.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: That's another. That, that's. That's very useful. No. Anything else you want to mention of like this particular case or within. Because you know already very well kind of the Aarhus landscape. Right. Of the. Of city government. Like, do you feel like there's traction or this is more kind of a PR thing to be like a global city or whatever, a larger influential city? How do you see this?

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: I think there is traction. I was reading before the interview, I was reading the new climate plan for the city. And it's very clear that from the research I did back in the. In the spring to now, food has become a major focus and it wasn't necessarily such a major focus before. So this is quite, quite a big accomplishment, I think for them. I don't. And maybe this is both Sadi's teaching but also just the illness talking in my head. I am not necessarily sure that focus on behavioral interventions will really be that successful. I can Understand why the Food Click team is trying to advise them to do this because they're in their scope 3 admissions and they want to really reduce how much the individual in the community is. Is. Yeah. Emitting. But I don't know how successful they're going to be if we have the same food landscape as we have right now. For example, in the supermarkets, if you want to buy non meats like meat substitutes, it's very expensive. It's something very new for the community. And being able to change this within two years without getting the full system on board or without getting subsidies to actually lower the cost of the substitute Substitute Subs why can't I say that word right now?

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Substitute. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Thank you. Yeah, I don't, I don't really see that working. Yeah. I know that academics are very fond of the idea of behavioral design and.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Yeah, it's a very kind of. Not only that, but you know, the governance of the project is very complicated because I mean, in the end what else are you going to do? You know, you don't have rule of law, which is what you actually have when you're government.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah. I'm also, I'm curious how much data they have to understand the rationality behind people's food choices. I didn't hear any data from Bender or Dan in relation to this. I'm sure there, there is data on why people buy what they buy. I'm not sure how detailed it is and therefore I don't know what they're basing their interventions on right now. As a citizen of Oz, I can see how the plant based narrative is being pushed on us a lot. But I can also see how the supermarkets aren't changing, the prices are not changing. I can imagine being a single mom of three and being like, I just don't. I can't afford this. Like this is not a possibility for me. My children aren't getting, getting, you know, fed properly. They. This is not going to work. And so I guess the food clicks holistic way of looking at the system is. It's preferable. But I'm not sure how they actually doing this holistic way of looking at it within the plant based narrative. Um, so I'll be interested to see how this works and if behavioral interventions can actually work to increase plant based living.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo
entries.text: Yeah, definitely. Following up can be interesting in the short future also because it's so contained, you know, like as he said, like this either happens between 2022 or 2027 and that's it. Or we just failed and we need to find different things. But either case you learn something. Right.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: I don't know if I mentioned it, but when you go back, I think one thing to ask is kind of the future plans because I wonder if within the next two years they're going to be preparing for this. And I would be really interested to see if Dan has a different idea of what he can actually do after, after the project ends. There might be some new opportunities because of the political landscape or the community support. Yeah. So I'm curious about that.

entries.speaker: Juan-Pablo

entries.speaker: Dan
entries.text: The name of the project is Foodclick. It's a project funded by the European Union in the Horizon Europe program. As part of Foodclick, we are Livinglab in Aarhus with the partners in the Livinglab being OHUS University with the researchers. And we are the Livinglab coordinators as Ohus municipality.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: And who are you? Could you please introduce yourself?

entries.speaker: Dan
entries.text: Sure. My name is Dan Christian Christensen. I'm a project leader in the Oaks Municipality and I'm responsible for our Living Lab coordination in the Food Click project.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: And how do you contribute to improving the food system here in August?

entries.speaker: Dan
entries.text: Hopefully we contribute to improving the food system in Owes through foodclick by having more capacity to link different stakeholders together. Also doing some of these real life interventions that are a huge part of FoodClick is hopefully also something that will spark some changes. But generally also bringing in the knowledge from the food cliq consortia. Having that as a backdrop in the development or the work we do in developing our own policies, for example, the climate plan and so on, I think has been hugely beneficial in terms of working with transforming the food system and making that part of our sort of ambition and priority as a political organization.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Perfect. Okay. And what would you like to achieve with food as a project?

entries.speaker: Dan
entries.text: Yeah, with Food Click as a project, we work with sort of an understanding of the food system as a holistic sort of interlinked system. So what I would like to achieve is to bring that sort of understanding into the work we do as a municipality in the future. Also, when we work with different policies in our context, that we take this perspective, that this understanding that the food system is sort of interrelated, it's quite complex and we need to sort of address it on multiple levels favorably. At the same time, bringing that into sort of our political organization, I think would be a huge success or a good benefit for also for creating traction on a better food system in the future. Yeah. In terms of obstacles, with natural encounters, sort of the complexity of linking together different areas of policy, many different people working with their own specific agendas and so on. That is a huge challenge and a huge obstacle for this more holistic transformation of the food system. That sort of people have a tendency, and also myself included, that you sort of focus on what you are, what's directly in front of you, what you are, directing responsibility and so on. But in terms to make influence on the food system, you need to sort of address things more on a cross cutting level. So that is the biggest obstacle, creating these sort of rooms, these sort of situations where you can create coordination between multiple agendas at the same time. That's an obstacle to create that in real life.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: And who do you work and collaborate.

entries.speaker: Dan
entries.text: With sort of directly in food? Click. We have a close collaboration with Auschwitz University, the Department of Food. They are sort of the living lab researchers in the project and we are the living lab coordinators. So we have a very tight, very close relationship and coordination and collaboration with them. Then also with the other living labs, we have sort of ongoing interactions with them to learn and to exchange ideas, so on. That's also close collaboration and good partners. And then we have the community that we have been allowed to at least develop and further sort of strengthen in owes with all these different stakeholders in the. In the food ecosystem. That's also close connections, collaborators that we. Yeah. Build up more knowledge about what are the priorities, what are the ambitions, what are the initiatives that people have and help them sort of align that with what we are doing as a municipality. So we collaborate quite broadly with the community that we have here in our context.

entries.speaker: Astrid
entries.text: Great.

entries.speaker: Dan
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.text: So do you have any advice to people who would like to do the same thing as Food Clickers did on.

entries.speaker: Dan
entries.text: Sure, there's a lot of advice that could sort of be relevant for people wanting to work with food system transformations, but from my perspective, sort of working in a public entity or with public administration, it would be to sort of be quite patient, be on the outlook, be on the lookout for opportunities to introduce food into other policy arenas. Being curious on how can you actually create value by introducing your perspective to other people, to other subject areas, because that's when you sort of create the value and also get traction on your own sort of ambitions to further the transformation of the food system.
